sections.heading: Active Voice
sections.body:
The subject does the action:

Ali (S) writes (V) a letter (O).
She (S) cooks (V) dinner (O).
They (S) built (V) the house (O).

Active voice is direct, clear, and preferred in most writing.

sections.heading: Passive Voice
sections.body:
The subject receives the action:

A letter (S) is written (V) by Ali.
Dinner (S) is cooked (V) by her.
The house (S) was built (V) by them.

Formula: Subject + be + past participle (+ by agent)

Use passive when:
• The doer is unknown: 'My bike was stolen.'
• The action matters more: 'The vaccine was developed in 2020.'
• Formal/scientific writing: 'The experiment was conducted.'

sections.heading: Forming Passive in All Tenses
sections.body:
Present Simple: is/are + past participle
'The book is read by many students.'

Present Continuous: is/are being + past participle
'The road is being repaired.'

Past Simple: was/were + past participle
'The letter was sent yesterday.'

Present Perfect: has/have been + past participle
'The project has been completed.'

Future: will be + past participle
'The results will be announced tomorrow.'

Modal: can/must/should be + past participle
'This form must be signed.'

jargon.definition: The subject performs the action. 'Ali writes a letter.' — Ali is doing the writing. Clear and direct.
The subject receives the action. 'A letter is written by Ali.' — The letter is the focus, not Ali. Used when the doer is unknown or unimportant.
The third form of a verb (V3). Regular: -ed (worked, played). Irregular: written, spoken, taken, done. Used in passive voice and perfect tenses.

quick.summary: Active = subject does the action (Ali writes). Passive = subject receives the action (The letter is written). Use passive when the doer is unknown or the action matters more than the actor.

sections.heading: When to Use Passive Voice
sections.body:
Use passive when:
1. Unknown doer: 'The window was broken.' (we don't know who did it)
2. Unimportant doer: 'The road was built in 1990.' (we don't care who built it)
3. Formal/scientific: 'The samples were analyzed.' (focus on process, not people)
4. Diplomatic: 'Mistakes were made.' (avoids naming who made them)
5. Emphasis on receiver: 'The President was awarded the prize.'

Avoid passive when:
• It makes sentences vague or wordy
• Active voice is more direct and engaging
• You're writing casually (emails, stories, conversations)

Comparison:
Active: 'I broke the window.' (clear, direct)
Passive: 'The window was broken.' (by whom? unclear)

sections.heading: Passive with Two Objects
sections.body:
Some verbs take two objects (indirect + direct):

Active: She gave me a gift.
Passive 1: I was given a gift (by her). ← More natural
Passive 2: A gift was given to me (by her).

Active: They sent him an email.
Passive 1: He was sent an email.
Passive 2: An email was sent to him.

Rule: The indirect object (person) usually becomes the subject in passive.

sections.heading: Common Passive Mistakes
sections.body:
1. Missing 'be':
❌ 'The letter written yesterday.'
✅ 'The letter was written yesterday.'

2. Wrong preposition:
❌ 'The book was wrote by her.'
✅ 'The book was written by her.'

3. Unnecessary 'by' for general agents:
❌ 'Mistakes are made by people.'
✅ 'Mistakes are made.' (obvious agent)

4. Passive + passive (double passive):
❌ 'The work was planned to be done by them.'
✅ 'They planned to do the work.'

5. Using passive with intransitive verbs:
❌ 'The accident was happened.' (happen has no object)
✅ 'The accident happened.'

sections.heading: Practice Conversion
sections.body:
Convert active to passive:
1. The teacher explains the lesson.
2. Someone stole my phone.
3. They will build a new hospital.
4. People speak English worldwide.
5. The company has launched a new product.

Convert passive to active:
1. The window was broken by the children.
2. English is spoken in many countries.
3. The project will be completed next week.
4. A new law was passed by the government.
5. The song has been downloaded millions of times.

deep.summary: Passive voice shifts focus from the doer to the receiver. Use it strategically for emphasis, formality, or when the doer is unknown. Overusing passive makes writing weak and vague.

javascript.code: Ali wrote the letter. The letter was written by Ali.

python.code: English is spoken in many countries.

comparisons.explanation: English follows consistent patterns once you learn the rules.

comparison.keyInsight: Understanding this concept is fundamental to English fluency.

questions.question: When should you use passive voice?
questions.answer: Use passive when: (1) the doer is unknown — 'My bike was stolen'; (2) the action matters more than who did it — 'The vaccine was developed in 2020'; (3) formal/scientific writing — 'The experiment was conducted'; (4) diplomatic language — 'Mistakes were made'. Avoid passive in casual writing where active is clearer and more engaging.

questions.question: How do you form passive in the present perfect tense?
questions.answer: Formula: has/have + been + past participle (V3). Examples: 'The project has been completed.' 'The files have been deleted.' 'She has been promoted.' The subject receives the action, and 'been' connects the perfect tense to the passive structure.

questions.question: Why is 'The window was broken' considered poor writing?
questions.answer: It's not necessarily poor — it depends on context. If you don't know who broke it, passive is correct. But if you do know, active ('John broke the window') is clearer and more direct. Passive without 'by' + agent leaves the reader wondering who did it, which can feel evasive or vague.

exercises.title: Active to Passive
exercises.description:
Convert these active sentences to passive:
1. The chef prepares the meal.
2. Someone broke the window.
3. They are building a new road.
4. The company will announce the results.
5. People have used computers for decades.
hints: Find the object — it becomes the subject in passive
Match the tense with the correct form of 'be'

solution.code:
1. The meal is prepared by the chef.
2. The window was broken (by someone).
3. A new road is being built.
4. The results will be announced by the company.
5. Computers have been used for decades.

exercises.title: Passive to Active
exercises.description:
Convert these passive sentences to active:
1. The book was written by a famous author.
2. English is spoken in many countries.
3. The report has been completed by the team.
4. A new bridge will be built next year.
5. The students were given extra homework.
hints: The 'by' phrase gives you the subject
If no 'by' phrase, add a logical subject

solution.code:
1. A famous author wrote the book.
2. People speak English in many countries.
3. The team has completed the report.
4. They will build a new bridge next year.
5. The teacher gave the students extra homework.

exercises.title: Active or Passive?
exercises.description:
Choose the best voice for each context:
1. Writing a news headline about a robbery: ___
2. Writing a story: 'I opened the door and ___'
3. Writing a scientific paper: 'The samples ___'
4. Giving a presentation: 'We ___ the product last quarter'
5. Writing an email: 'The report ___ by Friday'
hints: News/science often uses passive for objectivity
Stories and presentations use active for engagement

solution.code:
1. Passive: 'A bank was robbed yesterday' (doer unknown)
2. Active: 'I opened the door and saw a cat' (engaging narrative)
3. Passive: 'The samples were analyzed' (formal, focus on process)
4. Active: 'We launched the product last quarter' (direct, clear)
5. Passive: 'The report will be completed by Friday' (formal, focus on deadline)

sections.heading: Greetings and Introductions
sections.body:
Formal:
Good morning/afternoon/evening.
How do you do? (very formal, first meeting)
It's a pleasure to meet you.

Informal:
Hi! / Hey! / What's up?
How's it going?
Nice to meet you!

Introducing yourself:
I'm [name]. I work at [company].
I'm from [country]. I'm a [job].
I'm studying [subject].

sections.heading: At a Restaurant
sections.body:
Arriving:
A table for two, please.
Do you have a menu?

Ordering:
I'd like the chicken, please.
Can I have the pasta?
What do you recommend?
I'll have the soup and a salad.

During the meal:
This is delicious!
Could I have some water, please?

Paying:
Check/bill, please.
Can I pay by card?
Is service included?
Do you take cash?

sections.heading: Shopping
sections.body:
At a shop:
How much is this?
Do you have this in a different size/color?
Can I try this on?
Where are the fitting rooms?

Bargaining (where appropriate):
Is there any discount?
Can you make it cheaper?

At the cashier:
I'll take this one.
Do you accept credit cards?
Can I get a receipt?

jargon.definition: The amount you owe at a restaurant. 'Bill' is British English, 'check' is American English. Both are correct.
A paper or digital proof of purchase. 'Can I get a receipt?' — ask for it after paying.
To request food at a restaurant or goods at a shop. 'I'd like to order the chicken' or 'I'll take this.'
A scheduled meeting. 'I have a doctor's appointment at 3pm.' You 'make' an appointment (not 'do').

quick.summary: Daily English is about functional conversations: greetings, restaurants, shopping, phone calls. Memorize key phrases for each situation — fluency comes from knowing what to say.

sections.heading: Phone Conversations
sections.body:
Answering:
Hello? / Hello, [name] speaking.
Who's calling, please?

Leaving a message:
This is [name] calling for [person].
Could you ask them to call me back?
My number is [number].

Asking to hold:
Could you hold for a moment, please?
I'll put you through.

Ending:
I'll let you go. Talk to you later.
Have a good day! / Take care!

sections.heading: Doctor's Appointments
sections.body:
Making an appointment:
I'd like to make an appointment with Dr. [name].
I have an earache / headache / stomachache.

At the doctor:
What seems to be the problem?
I've had this pain for two days.
It started yesterday morning.
It hurts when I move my arm.
I feel dizzy / tired / nauseous.

Understanding instructions:
Take this medicine twice a day.
You should rest for a few days.
Come back if it doesn't improve.
Avoid heavy exercise this week.

sections.heading: Asking for Directions
sections.body:
Basic phrases:
Excuse me, where is the nearest...?
How do I get to [place]?
Is there a [bank/pharmacy/hospital] near here?

Understanding directions:
Go straight for two blocks.
Turn left/right at the traffic light.
It's on your left/right.
It's next to the supermarket.
It's opposite the park.
It's about a 10-minute walk.

Follow-up:
How far is it from here?
Can I take a bus/taxi there?
Is it walking distance?
Could you show me on the map?

sections.heading: Making Plans with Friends
sections.body:
Suggesting:
Do you want to grab lunch?
How about going to the cinema?
Shall we meet at 6?
I was thinking we could try that new restaurant.

Accepting:
Sounds great! / Sure, I'm in.
That works for me.
Let's do it!

Declining politely:
I'm sorry, I can't make it.
I have other plans. Can we reschedule?
I'd love to, but I'm busy that day.
Rain check? (Can we do it another time?)

Confirming:
So we're meeting at 7, right?
See you there! / Don't be late!

deep.summary: Daily English is functional — know the key phrases for each situation (restaurant, doctor, directions, phone, plans). Practice through role-play to build confidence.

javascript.code: Hi, how are you doing today?

python.code: Could you help me find the nearest hospital?

comparisons.explanation: English follows consistent patterns once you learn the rules.

comparison.keyInsight: Understanding this concept is fundamental to English fluency.

questions.question: How do you politely ask for something you need?
questions.answer: Use softening phrases: 'Could I...?', 'Would you mind...?', 'I'd like to...', 'May I...?'. For example: 'Could I have some water, please?' is more polite than 'Give me water.' Adding 'please' and a smile makes any request polite.

questions.question: How do you make small talk in English?
questions.answer: Start with safe topics: weather ('Nice day, isn't it?'), work ('How's work going?'), weekend plans ('Any plans for the weekend?'), or current events ('Did you see the game last night?'). Listen actively, ask follow-up questions, and share briefly about yourself. Avoid politics, religion, or personal finances.

questions.question: What should you say when you don't understand something?
questions.answer: Options: 'Sorry, could you repeat that?', 'I didn't catch that.', 'What do you mean by...?', 'Could you explain that again?', 'I'm not sure I follow.' Don't pretend to understand — it leads to bigger problems. Asking clarifying questions shows engagement, not weakness.

exercises.title: Restaurant Role-Play
exercises.description:
Write a complete restaurant conversation:
1. Greeting the waiter
2. Asking for the menu
3. Ordering food and drinks
4. Asking for the bill
5. Paying and leaving

solution.code:
Waiter: Good evening. A table for two?
You: Yes, please.
Waiter: Here's the menu.
You: Thank you. What do you recommend?
Waiter: The grilled chicken is popular.
You: I'd like the chicken, please. And a glass of water.
Waiter: Anything else?
You: No, that's all for now.
[After eating]
You: Could I have the bill, please?
Waiter: That's $25.
You: Here you go. Keep the change.
Waiter: Thank you. Have a good evening!
You: You too. Goodbye!

exercises.title: Asking for Directions
exercises.description:
You're lost in a city. Write the conversation:
1. You ask for the nearest subway station
2. The person gives directions
3. You ask a follow-up question
4. You thank them and leave

solution.code:
You: Excuse me, where is the nearest subway station?
Person: Go straight for two blocks, then turn left at the traffic light. It's next to the supermarket.
You: Is it far from here?
Person: No, it's about a 5-minute walk.
You: Thank you very much!
Person: You're welcome.

exercises.title: Making Plans
exercises.description: Write a text conversation where you suggest plans, your friend accepts, then one of you needs to reschedule:
hints: Use 'How about...?' or 'Do you want to...?' to suggest
Use 'I'm sorry, I can't make it' to politely decline

solution.code:
You: Hey! Do you want to grab dinner tonight?
Friend: Sure! Where should we go?
You: How about that new Italian place on Main Street?
Friend: Sounds great! What time?
You: How about 7pm?
Friend: Perfect, see you there!

[Later]
You: Hey, I'm so sorry but something came up. Can we reschedule?
Friend: No worries! How about tomorrow?
You: That works! Same time and place?
Friend: Deal!

sections.heading: Essay Structure
sections.body:
Every essay has 3 parts:

1. Introduction (1 paragraph)
   - Hook: grab attention with a fact, question, or quote
   - Context: brief background
   - Thesis: your main argument or point

2. Body (2-4 paragraphs)
   - Each paragraph = one main idea
   - Topic sentence → Evidence/Example → Explanation
   - Use linking words: however, furthermore, in addition

3. Conclusion (1 paragraph)
   - Restate thesis (different words)
   - Summarize key points
   - Final thought or call to action

sections.heading: Paragraph Writing
sections.body:
Each paragraph follows P.E.E.L:

P - Point: State your main idea
'Economic inequality is a growing global concern.'

E - Evidence: Support with facts or examples
'According to Oxfam, the richest 1% own more wealth than 99% of the world.'

E - Explanation: Explain how the evidence supports your point
'This statistic highlights the vast gap between the wealthy and the rest of society.'

L - Link: Connect to the next paragraph
'However, this issue is not limited to wealth alone.'

Tip: One paragraph = one idea. If you have two ideas, make two paragraphs.

sections.heading: Common Essay Mistakes
sections.body:
1. No thesis statement → essay lacks direction
2. Paragraphs too long → break them up (3-5 sentences each)
3. No examples → opinions without evidence
4. Repetition → say it once, say it well
5. Informal language → avoid contractions and slang in essays
6. Plagiarism → always cite your sources
7. Weak conclusion → don't just repeat; synthesize

jargon.definition: The main argument of your essay, stated in the introduction. It tells the reader what you'll argue. Example: 'Social media has more negative effects on teenagers than positive ones.'
The first sentence of a paragraph, stating the paragraph's main idea. It connects to the thesis. Example: 'One major benefit of exercise is improved mental health.'
Words that connect ideas: moreover, however, therefore, furthermore, additionally, consequently, in contrast, similarly.

quick.summary: Essays follow a clear structure: Introduction (thesis) → Body (P.E.E.L paragraphs) → Conclusion (synthesize). One paragraph = one idea, supported by evidence.

sections.heading: Types of Essays
sections.body:
1. Argumentative: Take a position and defend it
   'Should university education be free?'
   → Present your argument + evidence + counterargument

2. Explanatory: Explain a topic objectively
   'How does photosynthesis work?'
   → Inform, don't argue. Use facts and examples

3. Narrative: Tell a story
   'A time you overcame a challenge'
   → Use chronological order, dialogue, and detail

4. Descriptive: Paint a picture with words
   'Describe your favorite place'
   → Use sensory details (sight, sound, smell, touch, taste)

5. Compare/Contrast: Analyze similarities and differences
   'Online vs traditional education'
   → Use Venn diagram structure or point-by-point

6. Persuasive: Convince the reader to act
   'Why recycling should be mandatory'
   → Emotional appeals + logical evidence + call to action

sections.heading: Writing Strong Thesis Statements
sections.body:
Weak thesis: 'Social media is bad.' (too vague)
Strong thesis: 'Social media harms teenage mental health by increasing anxiety, reducing sleep quality, and promoting unrealistic body image standards.' (specific, arguable, 3 supporting points)

Weak: 'Education is important.' (obvious)
Strong: 'Project-based learning should replace standardized testing because it develops critical thinking, prepares students for real-world problems, and increases student engagement.'

Formula:
[Topic] + [Position/Claim] + [3 Reasons/Evidence]

Test: Can someone reasonably disagree with your thesis? If yes, it's arguable (good!). If no, it's a fact (not an essay topic).

sections.heading: Paragraph Development — P.E.E.L in Detail
sections.body:
P - Point (topic sentence):
'One significant advantage of remote work is increased productivity.'

E - Evidence (support):
'A 2023 Stanford study found that remote workers were 13% more productive than office workers, with fewer distractions and no commute time.'

E - Explanation (analysis):
'By eliminating the daily commute and office interruptions, employees can focus more deeply on their tasks, resulting in higher output and better work quality.'

L - Link (transition):
'However, while productivity benefits the employee, what about the company's perspective?'

Paragraph length: Aim for 4-6 sentences. Too short = underdeveloped. Too long = multiple ideas.

Linking words by function:
Addition: moreover, furthermore, additionally, in addition
Contrast: however, nevertheless, on the other hand, in contrast
Cause: therefore, consequently, as a result, thus
Example: for instance, for example, such as, to illustrate
Emphasis: indeed, in fact, notably, especially

sections.heading: The Writing Process
sections.body:
Step 1: Brainstorm (5 minutes)
   List all ideas related to the topic. Don't filter.

Step 2: Outline (5 minutes)
   Organize ideas into introduction, 2-3 body paragraphs, conclusion.

Step 3: Write first draft (20 minutes)
   Get ideas down. Don't worry about perfection.

Step 4: Revise (10 minutes)
   Check: Is the thesis clear? Does each paragraph have one idea? Is there evidence?

Step 5: Edit (5 minutes)
   Fix grammar, spelling, punctuation. Read aloud to catch errors.

Total time for a 500-word essay: ~45 minutes

Tip: Always leave time for step 4 and 5. A polished essay beats a longer rough one.

deep.summary: Master essay writing with structure: Introduction → P.E.E.L Body Paragraphs → Conclusion. Write strong thesis statements, support with evidence, and always revise before submitting.

javascript.code: Technology has changed our lives in many ways.

python.code: In conclusion, learning English is essential for career growth.

comparisons.explanation: English follows consistent patterns once you learn the rules.

comparison.keyInsight: Understanding this concept is fundamental to English fluency.

questions.question: What makes a strong thesis statement?
questions.answer: A strong thesis is specific, arguable, and previews your supporting points. Instead of 'Social media is bad' (too vague), write 'Social media harms teenage mental health by increasing anxiety, reducing sleep, and promoting unrealistic standards.' It takes a clear position that someone could disagree with, and lists 3 specific reasons you'll argue.

questions.question: How do you write a good introduction?
questions.answer: Start with a hook (surprising fact, question, or quote), provide brief context, then end with your thesis statement. The introduction should answer: 'What am I writing about?' and 'What is my main argument?' Keep it 3-5 sentences. Save the detailed evidence for the body paragraphs.

questions.question: What is the P.E.E.L structure?
questions.answer: P.E.E.L is a paragraph framework: Point (topic sentence stating the main idea), Evidence (facts, data, or examples), Explanation (how the evidence supports your point), Link (transition to the next paragraph). This ensures every paragraph is well-developed and connected to the overall argument.

exercises.title: Thesis Statement Practice
exercises.description:
Write a thesis statement for each topic:
1. Should homework be banned?
2. The benefits of learning a second language
3. Why cities should have more parks
4. Social media's impact on society
5. The importance of financial literacy
hints: Use the formula: Topic + Position + 3 Reasons
Make sure someone could reasonably disagree

solution.code:
1. Homework should be banned because it reduces family time, increases student stress, and shows no proven correlation with academic improvement.
2. Learning a second language improves cognitive abilities, enhances career opportunities, and fosters cultural understanding.
3. Cities should invest in more parks because they improve public health, reduce urban heat, and strengthen community bonds.
4. Social media has fundamentally changed communication by enabling global connectivity, but it has also increased isolation and mental health issues.
5. Financial literacy should be taught in all schools because it prevents debt, builds wealth, and creates economically responsible citizens.

exercises.title: P.E.E.L Paragraph
exercises.description:
Write a P.E.E.L paragraph about: 'Reading books is more beneficial than watching TV'
Include all four elements: Point, Evidence, Explanation, Link.
hints: Start with a clear topic sentence
Use a fact or example as evidence

solution.code:
Point: Reading books provides deeper cognitive benefits than watching television.
Evidence: A 2013 University of Sussex study found that reading for just 6 minutes reduces stress levels by 68%, while TV only reduces stress by 25%.
Explanation: This suggests that reading engages the mind more actively, requiring imagination and focus, while TV consumption is more passive and less mentally stimulating.
Link: Given these cognitive advantages, it's clear that reading deserves more of our leisure time.

exercises.title: Essay Outline
exercises.description:
Create an outline for a 5-paragraph essay on: 'Why every student should learn to code'
Include: Introduction with thesis, 3 body paragraphs, conclusion.
hints: Each body paragraph should have a distinct reason
The conclusion should restate the thesis in different words

solution.code:
Introduction:
- Hook: In today's digital world, coding is the new literacy.
- Thesis: Every student should learn to code because it develops problem-solving skills, prepares them for future careers, and fosters creativity.

Body 1: Problem-solving
- Coding teaches logical thinking and breaking complex problems into smaller steps.

Body 2: Career preparation
- Technology jobs are growing 2x faster than other sectors.

Body 3: Creativity
- Coding allows students to build apps, games, and solutions from scratch.

Conclusion:
- Restate thesis: Coding develops problem-solving, career readiness, and creativity.
- Call to action: Schools should make coding part of the core curriculum.

sections.heading: Tell Me About Yourself
sections.answer:
Use the Present-Past-Future formula:

Present: 'I'm a software developer with 3 years of experience at a fintech company, specializing in backend systems.'

Past: 'Before that, I graduated with a CS degree and worked as an intern at a startup, where I built my first production app.'

Future: 'I'm now looking to bring my skills to a larger team where I can work on scalable systems and grow as an engineer.'

Keep it 60-90 seconds. Focus on professional, not personal.

sections.heading: Common Interview Questions
sections.body:
Why do you want this job?
→ Show you've researched the company. Connect your skills to their needs.
'Your company's focus on sustainable tech aligns with my passion for building eco-friendly solutions.'

What are your strengths?
→ Give a strength + example
'I'm highly organized. At my last job, I managed a project tracker that kept our 10-person team on schedule.'

What is your weakness?
→ Be honest + show improvement
'I used to struggle with public speaking, so I joined Toastmasters and now I present at team meetings regularly.'

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?
→ Show ambition + alignment
'I aim to lead a development team and contribute to products that impact millions of users.'

sections.heading: Closing the Interview
sections.body:
Ask questions:
What does a typical day look like in this role?
What are the biggest challenges the team is facing?
How do you measure success for this position?
What are the next steps in the hiring process?

Closing statement:
'Thank you for your time. I'm very excited about this opportunity and confident I can contribute to your team.'

Follow up:
Send a thank-you email within 24 hours
Reference something specific from the conversation
Reiterate your interest

jargon.definition: A formal meeting where an employer evaluates a candidate for a job. Includes questions about skills, experience, and personality.
A document summarizing your education, work experience, and skills. CV (Curriculum Vitae) is longer and more detailed; resume is 1-2 pages.
Answering behavioral questions: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Structure your answer around a specific example.

quick.summary: Interview English is about structured communication. Use the STAR method for behavioral questions, research the company, and always ask thoughtful questions at the end.

sections.heading: The STAR Method for Behavioral Questions
sections.body:
Behavioral questions ask about past experiences:
'Tell me about a time you handled a difficult situation.'
'Describe a project you're proud of.'
'Give an example of when you worked under pressure.'

STAR framework:

S - Situation: Set the scene (1-2 sentences)
'In my previous role as a project manager, our main client requested a major scope change two weeks before launch.'

T - Task: What was your responsibility?
'I was responsible for managing the timeline and ensuring the team could accommodate the changes without delaying delivery.'

A - Action: What did YOU do? (Be specific)
'I organized an emergency meeting, broke the new requirements into smaller tasks, reprioritized the sprint, and brought in two developers from another team.'

R - Result: What was the outcome? (Use numbers if possible)
'We delivered on time with 95% of the new features. The client was impressed and signed a 2-year extension.'

Tip: Keep each STAR element to 2-3 sentences. Total answer: 60-90 seconds.

sections.heading: Professional Email Writing
sections.body:
Subject line: Clear and specific
✅ 'Application for Frontend Developer Position — Raj Singh'
❌ 'Job Application'

Opening:
Dear Mr./Ms. [Last Name], (formal)
Hello [First Name], (semi-formal if you've met)

Body:
• State your purpose in the first sentence
• Keep paragraphs short (2-3 sentences)
• Use bullet points for lists
• End with a clear call to action

Closing:
Best regards,
[Your full name]
[Phone number]
[LinkedIn URL]

Common phrases:
• 'I am writing to express my interest in...'
• 'I would like to follow up on...'
• 'Please find attached...'
• 'Thank you for your time and consideration.'

sections.heading: Negotiation Language
sections.body:
Salary negotiation:
'Thank you for the offer. Based on my research and experience, I was expecting a range of [X-Y]. Is there flexibility in the compensation package?'

Counter-offer:
'I appreciate the offer of $X. Given my [experience/skills/market rate], would you consider $Y?'

Non-salary negotiation:
'I'm excited about the role. Would it be possible to discuss [remote work/flexible hours/additional vacation]?'

Graceful acceptance:
'Thank you, I'm delighted to accept. I look forward to starting on [date].'

If declining:
'Thank you for the offer. After careful consideration, I've decided to pursue another opportunity that aligns more closely with my career goals. I wish you and the team all the best.'

sections.heading: Professional Vocabulary
sections.body:
Describing yourself:
Proactive, detail-oriented, team player, results-driven, adaptable, self-motivated, analytical, creative, reliable, collaborative

Describing achievements:
Spearheaded, implemented, optimized, streamlined, reduced, increased, launched, coordinated, facilitated, mentored

Describing skills:
Proficient in, experienced with, knowledgeable about, familiar with, competent in

Power phrases:
'I consistently exceed expectations'
'I thrive in fast-paced environments'
'I take ownership of my work'
'I'm passionate about continuous learning'

deep.summary: Interview English is a skill. Practice STAR answers for behavioral questions, write professional emails, and learn negotiation language. Preparation is the key to confidence.

javascript.code: I am a software developer with 3 years of experience.

python.code: I am passionate about building user-friendly applications.

comparisons.explanation: English follows consistent patterns once you learn the rules.

comparison.keyInsight: Understanding this concept is fundamental to English fluency.

questions.question: How do you answer 'Tell me about yourself' in an interview?
questions.answer: Use the Present-Past-Future formula. Present: your current role and key skills. Past: relevant experience and education. Future: why you're interested in this role and what you hope to achieve. Keep it 60-90 seconds, professional, and relevant to the job. Avoid personal details.

questions.question: What is the STAR method and when should you use it?
questions.answer: STAR stands for Situation, Task, Action, Result. Use it for behavioral interview questions like 'Tell me about a time when...' or 'Give an example of...'. Structure your answer: briefly describe the situation, explain your task/responsibility, detail the specific actions YOU took, and share the measurable result. Keep each element to 2-3 sentences.

questions.question: How should you answer 'What is your weakness?'
questions.answer: Be honest but strategic. Name a real weakness (not 'I work too hard'), show self-awareness, and explain what you're doing to improve. Example: 'I used to struggle with public speaking, so I joined Toastmasters and now I present at team meetings.' The key is showing growth, not perfection. Avoid weaknesses that are critical for the job.

exercises.title: STAR Method Practice
exercises.description:
Write STAR answers for these questions:
1. Tell me about a time you solved a problem at work.
2. Describe a situation where you had to meet a tight deadline.
3. Give an example of when you worked with a difficult team member.
hints: Keep each STAR element to 2-3 sentences
Use specific details and numbers in the Result

solution.code:
Example for Q1:
S: As a customer service rep, I received a complaint about a billing error that had persisted for 3 months.
T: I needed to resolve the issue and retain the customer.
A: I investigated the billing system, found a glitch in the auto-renewal code, escalated it to tech support, and followed up with the customer daily until it was fixed.
R: The issue was resolved in 5 days, the customer stayed, and my manager used my case as a template for the team.

exercises.title: Email Writing
exercises.description:
Write a professional email to:
1. Follow up on a job application (you applied 1 week ago)
2. Thank someone after an interview
3. Request a meeting with your manager about a promotion
hints: Subject line should be clear and specific
Keep the body short — 3-4 sentences max

solution.code:
1. Subject: Following Up — Frontend Developer Application
Dear [Hiring Manager],
I wanted to follow up on my application for the Frontend Developer position submitted last week. I remain very interested in the role and would welcome the opportunity to discuss how my skills align with your team's needs.
Best regards, [Name]

2. Subject: Thank You — [Job Title] Interview
Dear [Interviewer],
Thank you for taking the time to meet with me today. I enjoyed learning about [specific topic discussed] and am excited about the opportunity to contribute to [company goal]. Please don't hesitate to reach out if you need any additional information.
Best regards, [Name]

3. Subject: Meeting Request — Career Development
Dear [Manager],
I'd like to schedule a 20-minute meeting to discuss my career development and potential growth opportunities within the team. Would [date/time] work for you?
Best regards, [Name]

exercises.title: Interview Role-Play
exercises.description:
Practice answering these questions out loud (record yourself):
1. Why do you want to work here?
2. What's your greatest achievement?
3. Where do you see yourself in 5 years?
4. Why should we hire you?
5. Do you have any questions for us?
hints: For Q5, always have at least 2 questions prepared

solution.code:
Sample answers:
1. 'I'm drawn to [Company] because of your commitment to [specific value/project]. My experience in [skill] aligns with your team's focus on [goal].'

2. 'My greatest achievement was leading a team project that increased customer satisfaction by 25% in six months through a redesigned onboarding process.'

3. 'In 5 years, I aim to have grown into a senior role where I can mentor junior developers and lead impactful projects.'

4. 'You should hire me because I bring a combination of technical skills, problem-solving ability, and a track record of delivering results in fast-paced environments.'

5. 'What does success look like in this role in the first 90 days? What are the team's biggest challenges right now?'

sections.heading: What is Touch Typing?
sections.body:
Touch typing means typing without looking at the keyboard. Your fingers know where each key is by feel (muscle memory).

Benefits:
• 3-5x faster than "hunt and peck"
• Less eye strain (eyes stay on screen)
• Fewer errors (fingers know the layout)
• More productive (can think while typing)

The secret: Home Row. Your fingers ALWAYS rest on these keys:
Left hand: A S D F (index on F — note the bump)
Right hand: J K L ; (index on J — note the bump)
The bumps on F and J are your home position markers.

sections.heading: Finger Assignments
sections.body:
Each finger controls specific keys:

Left Hand:
• Pinky: 1, Q, A, Z, Shift, Ctrl
• Ring: 2, W, S, X
• Middle: 3, E, D, C
• Index: 4, R, F, V, 5, T, G, B

Right Hand:
• Index: 6, Y, H, N, 7, U, J, M
• Middle: 8, I, K, ,
• Ring: 9, O, L, .
• Pinky: 0, P, ;, /, Shift, Enter

Thumbs: Space bar (use whichever feels natural)

sections.heading: Practice: Home Row Only
sections.body:
Today's exercise: ASDF JKL;

Type these combinations:
1. asdf jkl; asdf jkl;
2. asdf asdf jkl; jkl;
3. fjdk slaj fjdk slaj
4. asdf fdsa jkl; ;lkj
5. a sad lad; a flask; a salad

Sentences:
"ask a sad dad"
"a lad asks a lass"
"fall as a fast lad"

Rule: NEVER look at the keyboard. If you make a mistake, backspace and retry by feel.

jargon.definition: Typing without looking at the keyboard, using muscle memory. Each finger is assigned specific keys.
The middle row (ASDF JKL;) where fingers rest. The tactile bumps on F and J mark the home position.
Words Per Minute. Standard measure: one word = 5 characters including spaces. Average typist: 40 WPM. Professional: 75+ WPM.
The ability to perform movements without conscious thought, developed through repetition. Key to touch typing.

quick.summary: Touch typing is a physical skill, not a mental one. Your fingers need to learn, not your brain. Practice 20 minutes daily, never look down.

sections.heading: The Science of Muscle Memory
sections.body:
Your brain stores motor skills in the cerebellum, not the conscious mind. This means:

1. Repetition is key: Do the same exercise 50+ times
2. Speed comes later: Focus on accuracy for the first 2 weeks
3. Errors compound: One wrong finger position affects all keys that finger reaches
4. Sleep helps: Your brain consolidates motor memories during sleep

Research shows: 10 minutes of focused daily practice beats 60 minutes once a week.

Timeline:
Week 1-2: Home row only, 15-20 WPM
Week 3-4: Add upper row, 20-30 WPM
Week 5-6: Add lower row, 30-40 WPM
Week 7-8: Numbers and symbols, 40-50 WPM
Week 9+: Speed building, 50-70+ WPM

sections.heading: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
sections.body:
Mistake 1: Looking at the keyboard
Fix: Cover your hands with a cloth. Or type in a dark room with only screen lit.

Mistake 2: Using wrong finger for a key
Fix: Print a keyboard map. Practice each finger's territory separately.

Mistake 3: Rushing speed before accuracy
Fix: Set a rule: if accuracy drops below 95%, slow down immediately.

Mistake 4: Not using all fingers
Fix: Pinky fingers are weak but essential. Practice pinky-only exercises.

Mistake 5: Inconsistent practice
Fix: Set a daily alarm. 20 minutes at the same time every day.

sections.heading: Ergonomics
sections.body:
Proper setup prevents injury and improves speed:

Chair: Feet flat on floor, knees at 90°, back supported
Desk: Elbows at 90°, wrists straight (not bent up or down)
Screen: Top of screen at eye level, 20-26 inches away
Keyboard: Tilted slightly away from you, spacebar near body

Wrist pain? You may be:
• Resting wrists on desk while typing (don't!)
• Bending wrists up (keep them straight)
• Gripping too hard (relax your hands)

Take breaks: 20-20-20 rule — every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds.

deep.summary: Touch typing is muscle memory built through consistent, focused practice. Start slow, maintain accuracy, and never look at the keyboard. Speed will follow naturally.

sections.heading: Common Patterns & Best Practices
sections.body:

1. Start Simple: Begin with the simplest implementation that works, then add complexity as needed.

2. Follow Conventions: Stick to established patterns and conventions in your codebase. Consistency makes code maintainable.

3. Test Your Work: Always test your implementation. Write tests that cover both happy paths and edge cases.

4. Document Decisions: Add comments that explain WHY, not WHAT. Future you (and teammates) will thank you.

5. Review and Refactor: Code reviews and regular refactoring keep code quality high.

sections.heading: हिंदी कीबोर्ड लेआउट
sections.body:
हिंदी में रेमिंग्टन या इन्स्क्रिप्ट लेआउट का उपयोग होता है। यह कोर्स रेमिंग्टन लेआउट सिखाता है (सबसे ज्यादा प्रचलित):

बाएँ हाथ की होम रो: फ, ख, घ, थ, छ
दाएँ हाथ की होम रो: ज, द, ग, म, न

उंगलियों की स्थिति:
• बाएँ हाथ की छोटी उंगली: फ
• बाएँ हाथ की अनामिका: ख
• बाएँ हाथ की मध्यमा: घ
• बाएँ हाथ की तर्जनी: थ, छ
• दाएँ हाथ की तर्जनी: ज, द
• दाएँ हाथ की मध्यमा: ग
• दाएँ हाथ की अनामिका: म
• दाएँ हाथ की छोटी उंगली: न

sections.heading: अभ्यास के शब्द
sections.body:
इन सरल शब्दों से शुरू करें:

एक अक्षर: फ, ख, घ, थ, छ, ज, द, ग, म, न
दो अक्षर: खघ, थज, छद, फम, खन, घम, थद
तीन अक्षर: खघम, थजद, छगन, फखघ

वाक्य:
मैं हिंदी सीखता हूँ
यह मेरा नाम है
क्या हाल है
अच्छा लगता है

sections.heading: अभ्यास के सुझाव
sections.body:
1. आँखें स्क्रीन पर रखें — कभी कीबोर्ड मत देखें
2. प्रत्येक कुंजी के लिए सही उंगली का प्रयोग करें
3. धीरे-धीरे शुरू करें: सटीकता > गति
4. रोज़ 15-20 मिनट अभ्यास करें
5. अपनी WPM और सटीकता को समय के साथ ट्रैक करें

पहले पाठ का लक्ष्य: प्रत्येक होम रो कुंजी को बिना देखे 10+ WPM और 90%+ सटीकता से टाइप करें।

jargon.definition: शब्द प्रति मिनट — टाइपिंग गति का मानक माप। एक शब्द = 5 अक्षर (स्पेस सहित)।
सही ढंग से टाइप किए गए अक्षरों का प्रतिशत। लक्ष्य: गति पर ध्यान देने से पहले 95%+।
सबसे लोकप्रिय हिंदी कीबोर्ड लेआउट, पुरानी रेमिंग्टन टाइपराइटर पर आधारित। अधिकांश भारतीय कार्यालयों में प्रयोग होता है।
कीबोर्ड की मध्य पंक्ति जहाँ उंगलियाँ विश्राम करती हैं। सभी कुंजियाँ यहीं से पहुँची जाती हैं।

quick.summary: सही उंगली की स्थिति से शुरू करें। कभी कीबोर्ड न देखें। पहले सप्ताह रोज़ 15 मिनट अभ्यास करें।

sections.heading: कीबोर्ड पर हिंदी को समझें
sections.body:
हिंदी टाइपिंग में ट्रांसलिटरेशन का उपयोग होता है — प्रत्येक हिंदी वर्ण एक विशिष्ट कुंजी से मैप होता है:

होम रो बाएँ हाथ:
फ (fa) — छोटी उंगली, ख (ka) — अनामिका, घ (gha) — मध्यमा, थ (tha) — तर्जनी, छ (cha) — तर्जनी

होम रो दाएँ हाथ:
ज (ja) — तर्जनी, द (da) — तर्जनी, ग (ga) — मध्यमा, म (ma) — अनामिका, न (na) — छोटी उंगली

मुख्य सिद्धांत: प्रत्येक उंगली का अपना 'क्षेत्र' है — वह केवल अपनी नियुक्त कुंजियाँ दबाती है। हाथ स्थिर रहता है; केवल उंगलियाँ चलती हैं।

sections.heading: उंगली गति अभ्यास
sections.body:
अभ्यास 1 — एकल उंगलियाँ:
बाएँ तर्जनी: थ थ थ थ, छ छ छ छ
दाएँ तर्जनी: ज ज ज ज, द द द द

अभ्यास 2 — दोनों हाथों का वैकल्पिक प्रयोग:
थज थज थज, छद छद छद
घग घग घग, खम खम खम

अभ्यास 3 — पूरी होम रो:
फखघथछ जदगमन
फखघथछ जदगमन

अभ्यास 4 — असली शब्द:
खघ, थज, छद, फम, खन
घम, थद, जन, मग, नथ

sections.heading: गति बढ़ाने के सुझाव
sections.body:
1. लय बनाए रखें: टूट-टूट कर नहीं, लगातार एक गति बनाए रखें
2. मेट्रोनोम ऐप का प्रयोग करें: 40 WPM से शुरू करें, हर सप्ताह 5 WPM बढ़ाएँ
3. एक ही पाठ को बार-बार अभ्यास करें जब तक वह मांसपेशी स्मृति न बन जाए
4. अपने सत्रों को रिकॉर्ड करें: WPM और सटीकता ट्रैक करें
5. सामान्य शुरुआती गलतियाँ:
   • कीबोर्ड देखना (इस आदत को तुरंत तोड़ें)
   • गलत उंगलियों का प्रयोग (यदि ज़रूरी हो तो शुरू से सीखें)
   • जल्दीबाज़ी (सटीकता 90% से नीचे गिर जाए तो धीमे करें)

साप्ताहिक लक्ष्य:
सप्ताह 1: 10 WPM, 90% सटीकता
सप्ताह 2: 15 WPM, 92% सटीकता
सप्ताह 3: 20 WPM, 94% सटीकता

deep.summary: सही उंगली की स्थिति सब कुछ है। इसे अभी ठीक करें, नहीं तो बुरी आदतें बन जाएँगी जिन्हें तोड़ने में महीने लगेंगे। लय से अभ्यास करें, गति से नहीं।

sections.heading: What is Upper Row Introduction?

sections.heading: Key Concepts
sections.body:
Upper Row Introduction is a fundamental concept that every developer needs to master. Here are the core ideas:

1. Core Principle: The main idea behind Upper Row Introduction is understanding how different components work together.

2. Application: In practice, you'll use Upper Row Introduction every day when building applications. It's one of those skills that becomes second nature.

3. Common Patterns: Most Upper Row Introduction implementations follow predictable patterns. Once you recognize them, you can apply them quickly.

sections.heading: What is Word Building Speed Test?
sections.body: Timed test of word-building skills before advancing. This lesson covers the essential concepts you need to understand to get started effectively.

sections.heading: Key Concepts
sections.body:
Word Building Speed Test is a fundamental concept that every developer needs to master. Here are the core ideas:

1. Core Principle: The main idea behind Word Building Speed Test is understanding how different components work together.

2. Application: In practice, you'll use Word Building Speed Test every day when building applications. It's one of those skills that becomes second nature.

3. Common Patterns: Most Word Building Speed Test implementations follow predictable patterns. Once you recognize them, you can apply them quickly.

sections.heading: Why This Matters
sections.body: Understanding Word Building Speed Test is crucial because it builds the foundation for everything that follows. Master this topic and you'll find subsequent concepts much easier to learn.

sections.heading: What is HTML?
sections.body: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for creating web pages. It describes the structure of a web page using a system of tags and attributes. Every website you visit is built with HTML at its core.

sections.heading: The Basic Structure
sections.body:
Every HTML document follows a standard structure:

1. DOCTYPE declaration: Tells the browser this is an HTML5 document
2. html element: The root element that wraps all content
3. head element: Contains metadata, title, and links to stylesheets
4. body element: Contains all visible content on the page

sections.heading: Your First HTML Page
sections.body:
Here's a minimal HTML document: My First Page

Hello, World!

This is my first web page.

jargon.definition: HyperText Markup Language - the standard language for creating web pages
A declaration that tells the browser which version of HTML the document uses

A special code element that defines the structure and content of a web page (e.g.,

,

,

)
A complete HTML component consisting of an opening tag, content, and closing tag

quick.summary: HTML is the foundation of every web page. Learn the basic structure including DOCTYPE, html, head, and body elements. Practice creating your first HTML document with proper structure.

sections.heading: Understanding HTML Document Structure
sections.body: HTML documents follow a hierarchical structure. Every document starts with a DOCTYPE declaration, followed by the html root element. Inside the html element, you'll find two main sections: head and body. The head contains metadata that isn't displayed on the page, while the body contains all visible content.

code.snippet:
Welcome to My Website

  • Home
  • About
  • Contact

Home Section

This is the main content area.

© 2026 My Website
code.explanation: This example shows a complete HTML5 document with proper structure. Notice the DOCTYPE declaration, semantic elements (header, nav, main, section, footer), and proper nesting.

sections.heading: Essential HTML Elements
sections.body:
HTML provides many elements for different purposes. Here are the most commonly used ones:

• Headings: h1 through h6 (h1 is the largest, h6 is the smallest)
• Paragraphs: p element for text blocks
• Links: a element with href attribute
• Images: img element with src and alt attributes
• Divisions: div element for grouping content
• Spans: span element for inline content

code.snippet:
Main Title

Subtitle

Section Title

This is a bold word and this is italic.

Visit Example Send Email

This paragraph contains a highlighted word.
code.explanation: These are the building blocks of HTML. Headings create hierarchy, paragraphs contain text, links connect pages, images display visuals, and divs/spans organize content.

sections.heading: HTML Attributes
sections.body:
Attributes provide additional information about HTML elements. They're placed inside the opening tag and usually come in name/value pairs.

Common attributes include:
• id: Unique identifier for an element
• class: CSS class name(s) for styling
• style: Inline CSS styles
• title: Tooltip text on hover
• data-*: Custom data attributes

code.snippet:
Page Title

Hover over this text to see the tooltip.
code.explanation: Attributes customize how elements look and behave. The id attribute uniquely identifies an element, while class allows multiple elements to share styles. Data attributes store custom information.

sections.heading: Best Practices
sections.body:
Follow these guidelines for clean, maintainable HTML:

1. Always declare DOCTYPE at the beginning
2. Use semantic elements (header, nav, main, article, section, footer)
3. Nest elements properly - never overlap tags
4. Include alt text for all images
5. Use lowercase for tag names and attributes
6. Quote all attribute values
7. Close all elements that require closing tags

sections.heading: Practice Task
sections.body:
Create a complete HTML page about your favorite hobby. Include:
• Proper DOCTYPE and html structure
• A header with navigation
• At least 3 sections with different content
• An image with alt text
• A footer with copyright information
• Use semantic elements throughout

deep.summary: HTML documents follow a structured format with DOCTYPE, html, head, and body elements. Master essential elements like headings, paragraphs, links, and images. Practice using attributes and semantic HTML to create well-structured, accessible web pages.

insights: Always include DOCTYPE declaration for consistent rendering across browsers
Use semantic HTML elements to improve accessibility and SEO
Provide meaningful alt text for informative images, empty alt for decorative ones
Associate labels with form inputs using the for/id pattern
Include viewport meta tag for responsive design

questions.question: What is the purpose of the DOCTYPE declaration in HTML?
questions.answer: The DOCTYPE declaration tells the browser which version of HTML the document is written in. In HTML5, it's simply <!DOCTYPE html>. It ensures the browser renders the page in standards mode rather than quirks mode, which provides consistent rendering across different browsers.

questions.question: What is the difference between the head and body elements in HTML?
questions.answer: The head element contains metadata about the document that isn't displayed on the page, such as the title, character set, viewport settings, and links to stylesheets or scripts. The body element contains all the visible content that users see and interact with on the web page.

questions.question: What are semantic HTML elements and why are they important?
questions.answer: Semantic elements clearly describe their meaning to both browsers and developers. Examples include header, nav, main, article, section, aside, and footer. They're important because they improve accessibility (screen readers can better understand page structure), SEO (search engines can better index content), and code maintainability (developers can quickly understand page structure).

questions.question: Explain the difference between block-level and inline elements.
questions.answer: Block-level elements (like div, p, h1-h6, ul, li) start on a new line and take up the full width available. They create new blocks in the layout. Inline elements (like span, a, strong, em, img) don't start on a new line and only take up as much width as necessary. They flow within the text content without breaking the layout.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the alt attribute in img tags?
questions.answer: The alt attribute provides alternative text for images. It serves multiple purposes: accessibility (screen readers read it for visually impaired users), SEO (helps search engines understand image content), and fallback (displays when images fail to load). For decorative images, use empty alt="" to skip them in screen readers.

questions.question: How do you create a hyperlink in HTML?
questions.answer: Use the anchor element with the href attribute: Link Text. The href attribute specifies the destination URL. You can add target="_blank" to open in a new tab, rel="noopener noreferrer" for security when using target="_blank", and title attribute for tooltip text.

questions.question: What are HTML attributes and where are they placed?
questions.answer:
Attributes provide additional information about HTML elements. They're placed inside the opening tag and come in name/value pairs. Example:

. Common attributes include id, class, style, title, href, src, alt, and data-* for custom data.

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the viewport meta tag.
questions.answer: The viewport meta tag controls how the page is displayed on different devices. The standard declaration is: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">. It ensures the page scales properly on mobile devices, prevents default zooming, and enables responsive design by setting the viewport width to the device width.

questions.question: What is the difference between id and class attributes?
questions.answer: The id attribute uniquely identifies an element (only one element per id per page) and is used for specific targeting in CSS and JavaScript. The class attribute can be applied to multiple elements and is used for grouping elements that share common styles or behaviors. IDs have higher specificity in CSS than classes.

questions.question: How do you ensure your HTML is accessible?
questions.answer: Key practices include: using semantic HTML elements, providing alt text for images, using proper heading hierarchy (h1-h6), ensuring sufficient color contrast, making all functionality keyboard-accessible, using ARIA attributes when needed, providing labels for form inputs, and testing with screen readers.

sections.heading: HTML Text Elements
sections.body: HTML provides many elements for formatting and structuring text content. Understanding these elements is essential for creating readable, well-structured web pages.

sections.heading: Headings and Paragraphs
sections.body: Headings (h1-h6) create hierarchy and structure. h1 is the most important, h6 the least. Paragraphs (p) contain blocks of text and automatically add spacing between them.

sections.heading: Text Formatting
sections.body:
HTML offers several elements for emphasizing and formatting text:
• strong: Important text (bold)
• em: Emphasized text (italic)
• mark: Highlighted text
• small: Smaller text
• del: Deleted text (strikethrough)
• ins: Inserted text (underlined)
• sub/sup: Subscript and superscript

jargon.definition: HTML elements (h1-h6) that define section titles and create document hierarchy
The p element that contains blocks of text content
Elements that convey meaning about their content (like strong vs just bold)

quick.summary: HTML text elements include headings (h1-h6), paragraphs, and formatting elements like strong, em, mark, and more. Use these to create well-structured, readable content.

sections.heading: Heading Hierarchy
sections.body: HTML provides six levels of headings, from h1 (most important) to h6 (least important). A well-structured document should have exactly one h1 per page, followed by h2 for main sections, h3 for subsections, and so on. Never skip heading levels (e.g., don't jump from h1 to h3).

code.snippet:
Main Page Title

First Major Section

Subsection of First Section

Another Subsection

Detail within Subsection

Second Major Section

Content under Second Section
code.explanation: This heading structure creates a clear document outline. Search engines and screen readers use this hierarchy to navigate the page.

sections.heading: Paragraphs and Line Breaks
sections.body: The p element wraps blocks of text. Browsers add spacing before and after paragraphs. For line breaks within a paragraph, use br (but use sparingly). For thematic breaks, use hr.

code.snippet:
This is the first paragraph. It contains multiple sentences that form a complete thought.

This is the second paragraph. Notice the spacing between paragraphs.

This paragraph has a
line break in the middle of it.

Content after the thematic break.
code.explanation: Paragraphs are the building blocks of text content. Use br only for line breaks within a paragraph (like poetry or addresses), not for spacing between paragraphs.

sections.heading: Text Emphasis and Formatting
sections.body:
HTML provides semantic elements for text formatting. Always prefer semantic elements over visual-only ones:
• strong: Indicates serious importance (renders as bold)
• em: Indicates stress emphasis (renders as italic)
• mark: Highlights text for reference
• small: Side comments, legal text
• del/ins: Shows document edits
• sub/sup: For footnotes, math,化学 formulas

code.snippet:
This is very important information.

This word is emphasized for stress.

The highlighted text is noteworthy.

This is fine print or a disclaimer.

The price was $99 $79 (20% off).

Water is H2O and E = mc2.

The chemical formula for sulfuric acid is H2SO4.
code.explanation: These elements add meaning to your content. Screen readers can announce emphasis, and search engines understand the relative importance of text.

sections.heading: Blockquotes and Citations
sections.body: Use blockquote for extended quotations and q for short inline quotations. The cite element marks up the title of a creative work, while the cite attribute on blockquote identifies the source.

code.snippet:
The only way to do great work is to love what you do.

As Steve Jobs said, Innovation distinguishes between a leader and a follower.

His latest book, The Innovation Mindset, was published in 2025.
code.explanation: Blockquotes are displayed as indented content. The cite attribute and element provide machine-readable citation information.

sections.heading: Lists: Ordered, Unordered, and Description
sections.body:
HTML supports three types of lists:
• ul: Unordered list (bullet points)
• ol: Ordered list (numbered)
• dl: Description list (term/definition pairs)

Nest lists by placing a new list inside a list item (li).

code.snippet:
• HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript
  1. Learn HTML basics
  2. Master CSS styling
  3. Build with JavaScript
  • Frontend
    • HTML

HTML
HyperText Markup Language - structures web content
CSS

JavaScript
Programming language for web interactivity
code.explanation: Lists organize content logically. Use ol for sequential steps, ul for non-sequential items, and dl for key-value pairs like glossaries.

sections.heading: Preformatted Text and Code
sections.body: The pre element preserves whitespace and line breaks. The code element marks up computer code. Use them together for displaying code snippets.

code.snippet:
function greet(name) {
    console.log(`Hello, ${name}!`);
    return true;
}

Use the console.log() function for debugging.
code.explanation: The pre element maintains formatting exactly as written. The code element semantically marks inline code. Together they're ideal for displaying code examples.

• Multiple paragraphs with appropriate text formatting
• At least one blockquote with citation
• An ordered list of steps
• An unordered list of features
• A description list for a glossary section
• Preformatted code example

deep.summary: Master HTML text elements including headings (h1-h6), paragraphs, emphasis (strong, em), lists (ul, ol, dl), blockquotes, and preformatted text. Use semantic elements to convey meaning, not just appearance.

insights: Always use semantic elements (strong, em) instead of visual-only tags (b, i)
Maintain proper heading hierarchy - never skip levels
Use lists (ol, ul, dl) for list content, not paragraphs with line breaks
Use blockquote for extended quotations, not div with manual styling
One h1 per page, followed by h2 for sections, h3 for subsections

questions.question: What is the difference between <strong> and <b> tags?
questions.answer: <strong> is a semantic element that indicates serious importance or urgency. Screen readers may emphasize this text. <b> is a visual-only element that makes text bold without adding semantic meaning. Always prefer <strong> for important content as it improves accessibility.

questions.question: How many h1 elements should a page have?
questions.answer: A page should have exactly one h1 element that describes the main content of the page. Multiple h1 elements can confuse screen readers and harm SEO. Use h2 for main sections, h3 for subsections, and so on.

questions.question: When would you use a description list (dl) instead of an unordered list (ul)?
questions.answer: Use a description list when you have term/definition pairs, key/value data, or glossary entries. The dl element contains dt (term) and dd (definition) elements. Use ul when you have a simple list of items without associated definitions or descriptions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the <pre> element?
questions.answer: The <pre> element preserves whitespace and line breaks exactly as written in the HTML source. It's typically used with the <code> element to display code snippets, poetry, ASCII art, or any content where formatting is important. Text inside <pre> is rendered in a monospace font by default.

questions.question: Explain the difference between <del> and <s> elements.
questions.answer: <del> indicates content that has been deleted from the document, often used with <ins> to show edits. It's semantic and meaningful. <s> indicates content that is no longer relevant or accurate but is kept for reference. Both render as strikethrough, but <del> has semantic meaning about document changes.

questions.question: How do you create a multi-level nested list?
questions.answer:
Place a new list (ul or ol) inside a list item (li) of the parent list. For example:
  • Item
    • Sub-item
. Each nesting level creates a new indentation. Most browsers add left padding/margin to nested lists.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the <mark> element?
questions.answer: The <mark> element highlights text for reference or notation purposes, like highlighting search results or important passages. It renders with a yellow background by default but can be styled with CSS. It's semantic and indicates the text is highlighted for the reader's attention.

questions.question:
When should you use
elements?
questions.answer:
Use
sparingly for line breaks within text where the break is meaningful, such as: • Poetry or song lyrics • Addresses • Chemical formulas • Lines that must stay together Don't use
for spacing between elements - use CSS margins/padding instead.

questions.question: How do you properly cite a source in HTML?
questions.answer: For block quotations, use <blockquote cite="URL">. For inline quotations, use <q cite="URL">. For the title of a work, use <cite>. The cite attribute provides a URL reference. For attribution, you can add a <footer> inside blockquote or a <cite> element after the quote.

questions.question: What are sub and superscript elements used for?
questions.answer: <sub> (subscript) displays text below the baseline, used for chemical formulas (H₂O), footnotes, and math. <sup> (superscript) displays text above the baseline, used for exponents (x²), ordinal numbers (1st), and footnotes. Both are inline elements that don't break the text flow.

exercises.solution:
Web Development Fundamentals

Introduction

Web development is essential in today's digital world. Every business needs a web presence.

This article covers the basics every developer should know.

Core Technologies

HTML

HTML provides the structure of web pages. It's the foundation of all web content.

CSS

CSS handles presentation and styling. It makes content visually appealing.

JavaScript

JavaScript adds interactivity and dynamic behavior to pages.

Best Practices

Always write clean, semantic code. Test across browsers and devices.

Last updated: January 2026

Use a description list for ingredients (dt for ingredient, dd for amount)
Use an ordered list for cooking steps
Include a blockquote for a cooking tip
Add proper paragraph text between sections
exercises.solution:
Classic Pancakes

These fluffy pancakes are perfect for weekend breakfast. Ready in just 20 minutes!

Ingredients

All-purpose flour
1½ cups
Milk
1¼ cups
Egg
1 large
Butter (melted)
3 tablespoons
Sugar
2 tablespoons

Instructions

  1. Mix flour, sugar, and baking powder in a large bowl.
  2. In a separate bowl, whisk milk, egg, and melted butter.
  3. Pour wet ingredients into dry ingredients and stir until just combined.
  4. Heat a griddle or pan over medium heat. Lightly grease.
  5. Pour ¼ cup batter for each pancake. Cook until bubbles form.
  6. Flip and cook until golden brown on both sides.

Tip: Don't overmix the batter - lumps are okay! Overmixing makes tough pancakes.

exercises.solution:
JavaScript Functions Guide

Basic Function

A function is created using the function keyword. Functions can accept parameters and return values.

function greet(name) {
    return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}

console.log(greet('World')); // Output: Hello, World!

Arrow Functions

ES6 introduced arrow functions with the => syntax. They're shorter and lexically scoped.

const add = (a, b) => a + b;

console.log(add(5, 3)); // Output: 8

Glossary

Function
A reusable block of code that performs a specific task
Parameter
A variable listed in the function definition
Return value
The value sent back by the function using the return keyword
Arrow function
A concise function syntax introduced in ES6

sections.heading: Images in HTML
sections.body: The img element embeds images into web pages. It's a self-closing tag that requires src (source) and alt (alternative text) attributes. Images can be in formats like JPEG, PNG, GIF, SVG, or WebP.

sections.heading: Video and Audio
sections.body:
HTML5 provides native video and audio elements:
• video: Embeds video content with controls
• audio: Embeds audio content with controls
• source: Specifies multiple media sources for compatibility

sections.heading: Responsive Images
sections.body:
Make images adapt to different screen sizes using:
• width/height attributes for aspect ratio
• CSS for responsive sizing
• picture element for art direction
• srcset for resolution switching

jargon.definition: Source attribute - specifies the path to the image file
Alternative text - describes the image for accessibility and when images fail to load
Design that adapts to different screen sizes and devices

quick.summary: HTML provides img for images, video for video content, and audio for sound. Always include alt text for images and use responsive techniques for different screen sizes.

sections.heading: The img Element in Detail
sections.body: The img element is a void element (no closing tag) that embeds images. Essential attributes include src (image URL), alt (description), width, height, and loading. The alt attribute is required for accessibility.

code.explanation: Always include alt text. Lazy loading improves performance for below-the-fold images. The figure/figcaption combination provides semantic meaning for images with captions.

sections.heading: Video Element
sections.body: The video element embeds video content with built-in controls. Use source elements to provide multiple formats for browser compatibility. Common attributes include controls, autoplay, loop, muted, and poster.

code.snippet:
<!-- Basic video with controls -->
<video controls width="640" height="360">
    <source src="movie.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    <source src="movie.webm" type="video/webm">
    Your browser does not support the video tag.
</video>

<!-- Video with poster image -->
<video controls poster="poster.jpg" width="640">
    <source src="tutorial.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    <track kind="subtitles" src="subtitles.vtt" srclang="en" label="English">
</video>

<!-- Autoplay muted video (for backgrounds) -->
<video autoplay muted loop playsinline width="100%">
    <source src="background.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
code.explanation: Provide multiple source formats for compatibility. The track element adds subtitles/captions. Autoplay requires muted attribute. Always include fallback text.

sections.heading: Audio Element
sections.body: The audio element embeds sound content. Similar to video, it supports multiple sources and provides playback controls. Use for podcasts, music, sound effects, and audio narrations.

code.snippet:
<!-- Basic audio player -->
audio controls>
    <source src="podcast.mp3" type="audio/mpeg">
    <source src="podcast.ogg" type="audio/ogg">
    Your browser does not support the audio element.
</audio>

<!-- Audio with preload hint -->
audio controls preload="metadata">
    <source src="song.mp3" type="audio/mpeg">
</audio>

<!-- Background audio (no controls) -->
audio autoplay loop hidden>
    <source src="ambient.mp3" type="audio/mpeg">
audio>
code.explanation: Audio works like video but is 1-dimensional. Preload hints help browsers decide whether to buffer the entire file. Hidden audio is useful for background sounds.

sections.heading: Responsive Images
sections.body: Responsive images adapt to different screen sizes and resolutions. HTML provides several mechanisms for this.

code.snippet: img src="photo-800.jpg" srcset="photo-400.jpg 400w, photo-800.jpg 800w, photo-1200.jpg 1200w" sizes="(max-width: 600px) 400px, (max-width: 1000px) 800px, 1200px" alt="Responsive image example"> picture> /picture> style> img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; } /style>
code.explanation: srcset lets browsers choose the best resolution. The picture element serves different images based on viewport width. CSS max-width ensures images don't overflow their containers.

sections.heading: IFrames for Embedded Content
sections.body: The iframe element embeds external content like maps, videos from other sites, or other web pages. Use the sandbox attribute for security.

code.snippet:
<!-- Embedded map -->
<iframe
    src="https://www.openstreetmap.org/export/embed.html"
    width="600"
    height="400"
    style="border: 0;"
    loading="lazy"
    title="Location map"
    allowfullscreen>
</iframe>

<!-- Embedded video -->
<iframe
    width="560"
    height="315"
    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/dQw4w9WgXcQ"
    title="YouTube video player"
    frameborder="0"
    allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture"
    allowfullscreen>
</iframe>
code.explanation: Iframes embed external content. Always include a title for accessibility. Use sandbox to restrict permissions. Loading="lazy" improves page performance.

sections.heading: Best Practices
sections.body:
Key guidelines for media elements:

1. Always provide alt text for images
2. Use multiple video/audio formats for compatibility
3. Include captions/subtitles for accessibility
4. Use lazy loading for below-the-fold images
5. Set explicit width/height to prevent layout shift
6. Optimize file sizes for performance
7. Use responsive techniques for different devices

sections.heading: Practice Task
sections.body:
Create a media-rich page that includes:
• At least 3 images with proper alt text
• A video player with multiple source formats
• An audio player for a podcast
• Responsive images that adapt to screen size

deep.summary: Master HTML media elements: img for images, video for video content, audio for sound. Always include alt text, provide multiple formats for compatibility, and use responsive techniques for different devices.

before.description: Missing alt, empty alt, redundant alt, overly verbose alt, missing alt

before.code:
<video src="movie.mp4"></video>

<video controls>

after.code:

    <source src="movie.webm" type="video/webm">

</video>

<!-- Background video (autoplay must be muted) -->
<video autoplay muted loop playsinline width="100%">
    <source src="background.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
after.description: Proper controls, multiple formats, fallback text, autoplay requires muted

before.description: Fixed widths, no responsive techniques, no resolution switching

after.description: Multiple responsive techniques for different use cases

before.code:
<iframe src="https://external-site.com"></iframe>
<iframe src="https://external-site.com" width="100%" height="500"></iframe>

after.code:

    sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin"

after.description: Sandbox for security, title for accessibility, lazy loading for performance

insights: Always provide meaningful alt text for informative images
Use empty alt="" for decorative images so screen readers skip them
Provide multiple video/audio formats for browser compatibility
Autoplay video requires the muted attribute
Use srcset and picture elements for responsive images
Always include a title attribute on iframes for accessibility

questions.question: What is the purpose of the alt attribute in img tags?
questions.answer: The alt attribute provides alternative text for images. It serves three main purposes: 1) Accessibility - screen readers read it for visually impaired users. 2) SEO - helps search engines understand image content. 3) Fallback - displays when images fail to load. For decorative images, use empty alt="" to skip them in screen readers.

questions.question: Why should you provide multiple source formats for video?
questions.answer: Different browsers support different video formats. By providing multiple sources (like MP4, WebM, and Ogg), you ensure compatibility across all browsers. The browser will use the first format it supports. This is similar to how font-face works with multiple font formats.

questions.question: What is lazy loading and when should you use it?
questions.answer: Lazy loading defers loading of resources until they're needed (typically when they enter the viewport). Use it for below-the-fold images and videos to improve initial page load performance. Add loading="lazy" to img and video elements. Don't lazy load above-the-fold content as it can hurt perceived performance.

questions.question: Explain the difference between img srcset and the picture element.
questions.answer: srcset provides multiple image resolutions and lets the browser choose the best one based on screen size and pixel density. It's for resolution switching. The picture element provides different images based on media conditions (like viewport width) and is for art direction - serving completely different crops or compositions.

questions.question: What security considerations apply to iframes?
questions.answer: Iframes can embed potentially malicious content. Use the sandbox attribute to restrict permissions (like scripts, forms, same-origin access). Always set a title for accessibility. Consider using loading="lazy" for performance. Avoid embedding untrusted content without sandbox restrictions.

questions.question: How do you prevent layout shift when loading images?
questions.answer: Always specify width and height attributes on img elements. This allows the browser to calculate the aspect ratio and reserve space before the image loads. You can also use CSS aspect-ratio property or wrapper elements with padding-bottom trick. Modern browsers use these dimensions to prevent cumulative layout shift (CLS).

questions.question: What is the poster attribute on video elements?
questions.answer: The poster attribute specifies an image to display before the video is played. It's like a thumbnail or preview image. Use a representative frame from the video. If not specified, the browser shows the first frame. Example: <video poster="thumbnail.jpg">. This improves user experience by showing something before playback starts.

questions.question: When would you use an iframe instead of directly embedding content?
questions.answer: Use iframes when: 1) Embedding content from another domain (like YouTube, Google Maps). 2) Isolating content for security (sandboxing untrusted content). 3) Embedding third-party widgets. 4) Displaying content that requires its own browsing context. Avoid iframes for same-origin content you control directly.

questions.question: How do you make images accessible to screen readers?
questions.answer: Key practices: 1) Always include meaningful alt text for informative images. 2) Use empty alt="" for decorative images. 3) Keep alt text concise but descriptive. 4) Use figure/figcaption for images with captions. 5) Don't start alt text with "Image of" or "Picture of" - screen readers already announce it's an image.

questions.question: What are the benefits of using WebP image format?
questions.answer: WebP offers: 1) Better compression than JPEG/PNG (25-34% smaller). 2) Supports both lossy and lossless compression. 3) Supports transparency (alpha channel). 4) Supports animation. 5) Widely supported in modern browsers. Use <picture> or srcset to serve WebP with fallbacks for older browsers.
